coredata.dc:title: Stable and efficient hybrid Ag-In-S/ZnS@SiO2-carbon quantum dots nanocomposites for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Hao
Yang, Jie
Liu, Yong-feng
Zeng, Fan-ju
Tang, Xiao-Sheng
Yao, Zhi-qiang
Guan, Hong-ling
Xiong, Qian
Zhou, Jia-er
Wu, Dao-fu
Du, Juan
coredata.dc:description: As a promising energy-saving technique, the eco-friendly and low-cost solid-state white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) based on quantum dots (QDs) have been widely studied. Herein, a WLED device prepared by core–shell structure nanocomposites based on Ag-In-S/ZnS@SiO2 quantum dots (AIS@SiO2) and carbon quantum dots (CDs) was successfully constructed. CDs were combined onto the surface of AIS@SiO2 QDs to synthesize Ag-In-S/ZnS@SiO2-Carbon quantum dots (AIS-CDs) nanocomposites with a white-light emission, which successfully overcome the quenching effect of CDs induced by conventional aggregation. The as-prepared AIS-CDs nanocomposites presented high stability and a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 35%. Moreover, the corresponding AIS-CDs nanocomposites-based WLEDs demonstrated the color coordinate of (0.32, 0.33), which is comparable to the pure white light (0.33, 0.33); furthermore, the luminous efficiency of the as-prepared WLEDs showed 15.1 lm W−1. These results reported herein may open up a new avenue for the development of high-performance, low-cost, and environmentally-friendly WLEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Nanocomposites
Ag-In-S/ZnS QDs
WLEDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2018YFB2200500 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61975023 , 61520106012 , 61674023 , 61875211 , 51702032 ); the Central Universities (2018CDYJSY0055, 106112017CDJQJ128837 , 2019CDYGYB010 , 2019CDYGYB019 , 2018CDQYDL0051 ); the Young 1000 Talents Program of China, Strategic Priority Research Program of CAS (Grant No. XDB16030400 ), the Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology ( cstc2018jszx-cyzdX0137 ); International S&T Cooperation Program of China ( 2016YFE0119300 ), Ministry of Industry and Information Technology “Green Manufacturing System Integration” Project ( 2018ZZCQ105 ), the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory of High Field Laser Physics (Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics), and the CAS Interdisciplinary Innovation Team.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic strategy of synthesis of AIS-CDs.

(a) TEM image of AIS@SiO2, and the inset shows the High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the surface of AIS@SiO2. (b) High-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) image and elemental mappings of AIS@SiO2. (c) TEM image of AIS-CDs. (d) HRTEM image of the surface of AIS-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern of the AIS, AIS@SiO2, CDs, and AIS-CDs. (b) FTIR spectra of the AIS, AIS@SiO2, APTES modified AIS@SiO2, AIS-CDs, and CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of the AIS-CDs. High-resolution XPS (b) Ag 3d, (c) In 3d, (d) S 2p, (e) Zn 2p, (f) Si 2p, (g) O 1s, (h) C 1s, and (i) N 1s.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs, AIS@SiO2, and AIS-CDs in liquid state (noted that the AIS@SiO2/CDs ratio of starting materials to prepare AIS-CDs nanocomposites is 20/1 in the experiment). (b) Fluorescence image of the CDs, APTES modified AIS@SiO2, AIS@SiO2, and AIS-CDs in solution. (c) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs, AIS@SiO2 and AIS-CDs in the solid state. (d) Images of the CDs, APTES modified AIS@SiO2, AIS@SiO2, and AIS-CDs powders under sunlight and UV light. (e) Variations of the PL intensities for the AIS-CDs powders versus annealing temperature. (f) Variations of the PL intensities for the AIS-CDs powders stored in an ambient room environment.

(a) EL spectra of the warm WLEDs, and the inset shows the pictures under the warm WLEDs. (b) EL spectra of the cold WLEDs, and the inset shows the pictures under the cold WLEDs. (Noted that the AIS@SiO2/CDs ratio of starting materials to prepare warm WLEDs and cold WLEDs is 20/0.42 and 20/1 in the experiment, respectively) (c) The CIE color coordinates and CCT of the warm WLEDs at different working voltages. (d) The CIE color coordinates and CCT of the cold WLEDs at different working voltages. (e) The CRI and luminous efficiency of the warm WLEDs at different working voltages. (f) The CRI and luminous efficiency of the cold WLEDs at different working voltages.
Comparison of the PLQY for some white-emitting nanocomposites and corresponding WLEDs with the published literatures.

row.entry: Zero- and two-dimensional hybrid CDs phosphors

head.ce:title: Stable and efficient hybrid Ag-In-S/ZnS@SiO2-carbon quantum dots nanocomposites for white light-emitting diodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a promising energy-saving technique, the eco-friendly and low-cost solid-state white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) based on quantum dots (QDs) have been widely studied. Herein, a WLED device prepared by core–shell structure nanocomposites based on Ag-In-S/ZnS@SiO2 quantum dots (AIS@SiO2) and carbon quantum dots (CDs) was successfully constructed. CDs were combined onto the surface of AIS@SiO2 QDs to synthesize Ag-In-S/ZnS@SiO2-Carbon quantum dots (AIS-CDs) nanocomposites with a white-light emission, which successfully overcome the quenching effect of CDs induced by conventional aggregation. The as-prepared AIS-CDs nanocomposites presented high stability and a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 35%. Moreover, the corresponding AIS-CDs nanocomposites-based WLEDs demonstrated the color coordinate of (0.32, 0.33), which is comparable to the pure white light (0.33, 0.33); furthermore, the luminous efficiency of the as-prepared WLEDs showed 15.1 lm W−1. These results reported herein may open up a new avenue for the development of high-performance, low-cost, and environmentally-friendly WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) as a promising illumination source have evoked considerable attention in lighting and display fields, which stems from their long operational life, high efficiency, low power consumption, safety, and compact design merits [1–4]. At present, the principal strategies for commercial WLEDs mainly focus on combining blue or ultraviolet LED chip with yellow or multi-color emitting phosphors [5–7]. This type of WLEDs is widely used due to its high efficiency and compact property. However, it still suffers from high chromatic aberration, poor color stability, and poor color rendering index (CRI), caused by the different luminous efficiency of phosphors and chip under the same voltage [8,9]. In addition, the limited global reservation and ever-increasing price of rare-earth elements in the phosphors have further hindered the application of this WLEDs [10–12]. Developing quantum dots-based white light emitting systems is recognized as one alternative strategy because of their good chemical stability and excellent optical properties of quantum dots [13–15]. For example, Jang et al, reported CdSe QDs-based WLEDs with a luminous efficiency of 41 lm W−1 [16]. Nevertheless, the Cd2+-based QDs materials are hazardous and would deteriorate the quality of global water resources and environment [17,18]. It is, therefore, highly desirable to explore low-cost, high quality, and eco-friendly phosphors for the development of next-generation WLEDs.

In recent years, the Ag-In-S/ZnS (AIS) QDs as a new class of phosphor material have been studied extensively due to their distinctive advantages such as tunable bandgap, low toxicity and high quantum yields compared with conventional photoluminescent materials [19–21], which make them suitable as optical materials for the design of environmentally-friendly WLEDs. In particular, the wide bandwidth emission of AIS QDs enable them the potential application in constructing high CRI WLEDs based on tricolor (RGB)-emitting phosphors. However, the AIS QDs generally present a high PLQY in yellow and red region [22,23]; it is still a big challenge to synthesize blue emissive AIS QDs with a high PLQY [24]. As it known that, the weak blue light in the blue spectral region of the WLEDs would lead to a poor CRI. Fortunately, carbon quantum dots (CDs), as one branch of carbon nanomaterials, have been acknowledged as one of the most outstanding fluorescent materials owing to their good photostability, high thermal stability, and environmental friendliness [25,26]. Especially, blue emissive CDs with a high PLQY of 80% were reported by Yang’s group [27], which make them ideal candidates for fabrication of high CRI WLEDs [28–30]. Nevertheless, many studies have confirmed that the solid-state CDs usually showed photoluminescence quenching as the reason of excessive resonance energy transfer due to self-aggregation [25,31]. Obviously, the self-aggregation of solid-state CDs issue has greatly limited the commercial application of this novel fluorescent materials in WLEDs. In general, some research groups disperse CDs in inorganic salts [32], poly (vinyl alcohol) [31], starch [33], PMMA or other solid-state matrices [34] to avoid the aggregation phenomenon. But such CDs-based composites always show relative low fluorescence, owing to the low concentration of the luminescent CDs in the composites [35]. Furthermore, those composites usually suffer from low efficiency and stability problems. On the other side, the aggregation of CDs would induce quenching phenomenon when the space of two adjacent CDs is close to the Förster distance (R0) as the Förster resonance energy transfer effect [36,37]. Therefore, how to combine the high efficiency AIS QDs and blue CDs is important for preparing high CRI WLEDs.

In this work, we reported the novelty synthesis process for preparing core–shell nanocomposites with intrinsic white light by electrostatic adsorption between positively charged AIS QDs and negatively charged CDs. Growing CDs onto the surface of AIS QDs could fix the interspace between the neighbouring CDs, thus markedly preventing the aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) [33,38]. In addition, benefiting from this unique structure, the solid-state white-light emission is achieved by the combination of blue light from the CDs and the yellow light from the AIS QDs. And the PLQY of the intrinsic white light generating nanocomposites is 35%. Furthermore, the low-cost and environmentally-friendly nanocomposites were used as down-conversion fluorescent materials on UV chips, and the corresponding WLEDs were successfully fabricated with a high CRI of 91 and an CCT of 6021 k. The optimized CIE index of the fabricated WLEDs is located at (0.32, 0.33), which is close to standard white light (0.33,0.33). Additionally, the luminous efficiency of the as-developed WLEDs can reach 15.1 mW−1.

Fig. 1 schematically illustrates the synthesis route of the AIS-CDs composites, which involves three sequential steps. Firstly, the AIS QDs and CDs are prepared according to previous works [23,29]. A representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-prepared AIS QDs and CDs is shown in Fig. S1. From the Fig. S1, it could be observed that the AIS QDs show clear crystalline with the size distribution from 3 nm to 6 nm, whereas the CDs is about 5 nm. Secondly, we employ the modified Stöber method to prepare AIS@SiO2 composites [39]. From the TEM images of AIS@SiO2 shown in Fig. 2 a, the AIS QDs are almost totally embedded into the silica sphere with a smooth surface, and nearly no any nanoparticles could be observed on the surface (inset of Fig. 2a). In addition, the distribution of AIS QDs inside the SiO2 sphere could be observed clearly from the HAADF-STEM images (Fig. 2b). The elemental mapping shows that the Ag, In, S, Zn elements are uniformly distributed in the SiO2 sphere. Moreover, As shown in the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) of the AIS@SiO2 (Fig. S2), all the Ag, In, S, Zn and Si elements could be detected, confirming the AIS QDs are successfully embedded into the silica sphere.

From the zeta potential measurements demonstrated in Fig. S3, the surface of the AIS@SiO2 show negative charge, consistent with previous reports [40], which can be attributed to the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of silica shell. In addition, the CDs are also found to be negatively charged according to previous report [41]. Due to the same surface charge, it is difficult to directly decorate CDs onto the surface of the AIS@SiO2 upon electrostatic attractions in the subsequent formation step. In order to obtain a positive zeta potential of silica shells, we functionalized the AIS@SiO2 by the APTES that can be covalently link to the SiO2 surface [42], making it possible to decorate CDs onto the surface of SiO2. As confirmed from the zeta potential measurements on the surface of the AIS@SiO2 in the water (Fig. S3), the negative surface charge has been successfully converted to positive due to the functionalization with APTES.

Finally, the AIS-CDs composites are synthesized by simply stirring the CDs solution and APTES modified AIS@SiO2 in deionized water. Because of the electrostatic attractions between the positively charged APTES-functionalized SiO2 sphere surface and the negatively charged CDs, CDs are successfully attached onto the surface of the AIS@SiO2, as shown in Fig. 2c. In contrast with the smooth surface of AIS@SiO2, the AIS-CDs composites present a coarse surface, which are densely covered by CDs attached to the silica sphere. Besides, as shown in the HRTEM image of AIS-CDs composites (Fig. 2d), it could be observed that the CDs are dispersed on the surface of AIS@SiO2. As a result, a nanocomposite with a core–shell structure based on AIS QDs and CDs is formed in this whole process.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of the as-prepared CDs, AIS QDs, AIS@SiO2 and AIS-CDs composites are demonstrated in Fig. 3 a, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 27.2°, 45.5° and 52.7°, which can be attributed to (1 1 2), (2 2 0) and (3 1 2) planes of tetragonal chalcopyrite crystal structure (JCPDS no. 25-1330) [43], have been observed in AIS-CDs composites. Another additional broad peak appearing at around 22° can be assigned to (0 0 2) the phase of the amorphous carbon and silicon dioxide by comparing with the CDs and AIS@SiO2, respectively. The surface structure of the AIS QDs, AIS@SiO2, APTES modified AIS@SiO2, AIS-CDs composites, CDs are further studied by FTIR spectra, as shown in Fig. 3b. From the FTIR spectrum of the AIS QDs, the strong characteristic peaks located at 793, 954, and 1056 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of AIS@SiO2 can be assigned to the formation of the SiO2 shell. In detail, the 1056 and 793 cm−1 modes can be assigned to the Si-O-Si bond stretching vibrations, whereas the absorption band at 954 cm−1 corresponds to Si–O–H bending vibrations. In addition, the 2912 and 1463 cm−1 modes appearing in the FTIR spectra of the APTES modified AIS@SiO2 correspond to stretching vibrations related to C–H and N–H bond of amine functional group, indicating the successful functionalization with APTES [44]. Comparing to the AIS@SiO2 and CDs, the variations ranging from 3420 to 723 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of the AIS-CDs composites can be attributed to the results of decorating CDs onto the surface of AIS@SiO2. All these findings indicate that the AIS-CDs composites are consisted of CDs and AIS@SiO2.

Detailed elemental analysis and surface structure of the AIS-CDs composites have been performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements, as shown in Fig. 4 . The peaks including Ag 3d, In 3d, Zn 2p, S 2p, O 1s, Si 2p, C 1s and N 1S appear simultaneously (Fig. 4a). The high-resolution XPS surveys reveal the valence bond of the AIS-CDs composites. As show in Fig. 4(b-e), the XPS spectra in the ranges of Ag 3d, In 3d, S 2p, and Zn 2p show corresponding doubles for Ag+ (366.9/372.9 eV), In3+ (443.9/451.5 eV), S2− (160.9/161.9 eV), and Zn2+ (1020.6/1043.6 eV), respectively, which can be attributed to the existence of AIS QDs. In addition, the characteristic peak located at 103.2 and 532.1 eV for Si-O band, revealing the presence of SiO2 layer (Fig. 4f and Fig. 4g). The existence of CDs is also revealed by the C 1s XPS spectrum analysis. Distinguishable peaks at 284.1, 285.6, and 287.4 eV related to CC/CC, CO/CO, and CN (Fig. 4h), respectively, which stem from the CDs of the AIS-CDs. The N 1s contained two peaks at 399.2 and 400.9 eV for graphitic N and amino N, respectively, as show in Fig. 4i. These results imply that the CDs have been successfully decorated onto the surface of the AIS@SiO2 to form AIS-CDs nanocomposites.

The normalized absorption spectra of CDs, AIS QDs, AIS@SiO2 and AIS-CDs composites (noted that the AIS@SiO2/CDs ratio of starting materials to prepare AIS-CDs nanocomposites is 20/1 in the experiment) are presented in Fig. S4. The CDs show two absorption peaks at 248 nm and 353 nm, and the tail extends to 600 nm. According to previous reports [29], the absorption peak at about 248 nm relates to the aromatic rings of carbon cores, while the absorption band located at 353 nm corresponds to the surface states. A strong and broad absorption band from 253 nm to 600 nm could be observed in the absorption spectra of AIS@SiO2, which is similar to the AIS QDs. In the case of AIS-CDs composites, the absorption spectra is similar to that of CDs in shape. But the intensity shows slight variation, which can be attributed the absorption of AIS@SiO2. The fluorescence spectra of CDs, AIS@SiO2, and AIS-CDs in the liquid state are presented in Fig. 5 a. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the CDs have a maximum PL emission peak at 456 nm (full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 78 nm) with a PLQY of 63% and the PL peak of AIS@SiO2 is located at 573 nm (FWHM of 128 nm) with a PLQY of 44%. Both the AIS@SiO2 and CDs present a broader FWHM than traditional rare-earth phosphors, resulting in spectral overlap between their emitted light, which is of a great benefit to construct the WLEDs with a high CRI. As shown green line in Fig. 5a, the emission spectrum of the as-prepared AIS-CDs composites demonstrates two peaks. One is similar with the emission of CDs located at ~456 nm, and another is located at ~573 nm, almost identical with the excitonic emission band of AIS@SiO2. Therefore, the fluorescence emission from the nanocomposites owe to the contribution of both AIS@SiO2 and CDs. Also, the corresponding fluorescence images of the materials in the liquid state are shown in Fig. 5b. Bright blue and yellow light emission are observed from CDs and AIS@SiO2 under an UV irradiation of 365 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence of APTES modified AIS@SiO2 is similar to that of AIS@SiO2; but the as-prepared AIS-CDs nanocomposites present intense white emission, which confirm that the white light can be achieved from the nanocomposites. Furthermore, the PLQY of the white light emission from the nanocomposites could reach 35%, which is comparable to most of those CDs-based nanocomposites with white-light emission [31,41,45–50], as shown in Table 1 .

In the following, we will investigate the emission property of the as-prepared AIS-CDs composites in the solid state, because solid-state powders are usually essential for applications as fluorescent materials in WLEDs. According to the strategy we proposed, CDs are deposited onto the surface of AIS@SiO2 to form AIS-CDs nanocomposites. In experiment, the AIS-CDs powders are obtained by drying the corresponding precipitates in a vacuum oven. The emission spectra of AIS@SiO2 powders are presented in Fig. 5c. For comparison, the optical properties of CDs and AIS@SiO2 powder are also shown. Noted that under same excitation condition, the AIS@SiO2 powders show same emission spectrum compared with the AIS@SiO2 solution, but the emission of CDs powders is nearly vanished. The phenomenon of losing fluorescence from CDs in the solid states could be attributed to the AIQ, which has been frequently reported [27]. In the case of AIS-CDs powders, the strong emission contributed from both AIS@SiO2 (573 nm) and CDs (456 nm) has been observed clearly, which is nearly identical to the emission of AIS@SiO2 and CDs solutions. In addition, the fluorescence photographs of CDs, APTES modified AIS@SiO2, AIS@SiO2, and AIS-CDs composites powders under room light and UV lamp are presented in Fig. 5d. The CDs powders are black in color, while both APTES modified AIS@SiO2 and AIS@SiO2 powders are pale yellow under the room light conditions; for the AIS-CDs powders, they are white in color. Under the irradiation of UV lamp, the CDs powder show almost no fluorescence due to the fluorescence quenching, whereas the APTES modified AIS@SiO2 and AIS@SiO2 both exhibit bright yellow-light emission; most importantly, the AIS-CDs also show bright white-light emission. The above results further confirm that CDs have been deposited on the surface of AIS@SiO2 to form AIS-CDs nnanocomposites, and the surface of AIS@SiO2 can act as the spacer to prohibit the CDs aggregation in the solid-state. The thermal stability of the AIS-CDs powders has been investigated through annealing the composites at different temperature. As shown in Fig. 5e, the temperature-dependent PL intensity of the emission band at 456 nm and 573 nm is investigated in the range from 30 to 130 °C. It is clear that the intensity of the two emission bands remains almost unchanged with the elevated temperature, which suggests the excellent temperature tolerance of the AIS-CDs composites. Furthermore, the stability of the AIS-CDs composites at different stored times under ambient conditions has been evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5f, the intensity of two emission peaks are nearly the same as the beginning value after being stored for 720 h. The abovementioned data suggest that the AIS-CDs composites powders are expected to be a kind of promising intrinsic white-light-emitting material in WLEDs applications.

Additionally, the energy transfer might occur from CDs to AIS@SiO2 in the AIS-CDs composites due to the spectrum overlap between the blue emission of CDs and the absorption of AIS@SiO2, which can cause poor color stability and luminous efficiency of the composites-based WLEDs. The energy transfer mechanism between CDs and AIS@SiO2 is study by the analysis of time-resolved PL spectra decays of the CDs in the AIS-CDs composites. As exhibited in Fig. S5, the average lifetime of CDs decreases slightly from 14.65 ns to 14.54 in AIS-CDs composites. It suggests that the PL decay of CDs becomes faster when introducing AIS@SiO2, confirming that the energy of CDs would transfer to the AIS@SiO2. The energy transfer efficiency among AIS@SiO2 and CDs are calculated by using the following equation:
φ = 1 -
∫
PL
D 1
E
d E
∫
PL
D 2
E
d E
= 1 -
τ 1
τ 2
where
φ i s
the transfer efficiency,
τ
2
is the average PL lifetime of the donor in the absence of acceptor, and
τ
1
is the average PL lifetime of donor in the presence of acceptor [51,52]. As a result, the calculated energy transfer efficiency of the composites is only about 0.8%, indicating the energy transfer between the AIS@SiO2 and CDs can be negligible, and their optical properties have hardly changed in the as-fabricated WLEDs.

Benefiting from its unique structure and performance, the corresponding WLEDs are constructed by the integration of AIS-CDs composites and a UV chip of 365 nm. In this case, PMMA is used as encapsulation materials owing to its high transmission. The as-prepared AIS-CDs powder were mixed with PMMA in acetone, and then dropped onto the UV chips to form down-conversion phosphors. Additionally, in the specific process of synthesizing AIS-CDs nanocomposites, the CDs and modified AIS@SiO2 are stirred overnight in order to achieve electrostatic attractions, which enable CDs attach onto the surface of AIS@SiO2. Therefore, we could obtain nanocomposites containing different amounts of CDs, and accordingly the different blue/yellow PL intensity ratios of the prepared nanocomposites by simply regulating the doping amount of the CDs. As a result, the electroluminescence spectra of the WLEDs prepared by white-light-emitting nanocomposites could be adjusted by the intensity ratio of two major peaks. The white-light emission could become colder by increasing the blue component, and on the contrary it could tune to warmer. Fig. 6 displays the characteristics of the two constructed WLEDs devices. The EL emission spectra of the two devices at different working voltage from 3.2 to 3.45 V are shown in Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b, covering the band of blue, yellow and red well, which results in a positive impact on CRI; and two peaks located at around 456 nm and 573 nm can be observed clearly. Noted that the emission bands located at ~456 nm and ~573 nm are corresponding to the fluorescence of CDs and AIS@SiO2 under the excitation of UV chips, respectively. Also, The EL intensity of both WLEDs devices increase with the forward voltage, indicating no saturation takes place. Moreover, the white-light emission could change from warm to cold if we increase the amount of blue light component (inset of Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b). As shown in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, the CIE color coordinate and CCT of the warm WLEDs change slightly from (0.37, 0.39) to (0.38, 0.40) and from 4248 to 3856 K as the forward voltage increase from 3.2 to 3.45 V, while the cold WLEDs shift from (0.32, 0.33) to (0.32, 0.34) and from 6338 to 5840 K, respectively. The voltage-dependent luminous efficiency and CRI of the warm WLEDs devices are also studied. As presented in Fig. 6e, with the increase of the working voltage, the CRI remains almost constant, and the luminous efficiency (LE) shows a slight variation. In the case of the cold WLEDs (Fig. 6f), the CRI is as high as 91, significantly exceeding the value (CRI ≈ 82) of commercial WLEDs based on the inorganic phosphors; while increasing working voltage, the LE of the device change slightly, reaching 16.8 lm W−1 as the voltage rise to 3.25 V. All the results indicate the good color stability of the fabricated WLEDs, which is consistent with excellent thermal stability of the prepared AIS-CDs nanocomposites. From the Fig. S6, the EL spectrum of the as-fabricated cold WLEDs almost remains unchanged after operating 15 h at 3.4 V, and the CCT and CRI are also nearly constant. For the LE, it still maintains about 85% of the initial value, indicating the optical stability of the WLEDs. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1, we summarized some published reports about the WLEDs based on white-light-emitting nanocomposites in order to evaluate the performance level of the as-constructed WLEDs. The CRI is found to the highest among the reported literature. The above results demonstrate that the WLEDs based on AIS-CDs nanocomposites have the potential to be the lighting source in illumination systems.

In summary, we reported the synthesis of white-light-emitting AIS-CDs nanocomposites with a core–shell structure, in which AIS@SiO2 can act as the spacer to prohibit the aggregation of CDs in solid state. Detailed characterizations suggest that CDs are dispersed onto the surface of AIS@SiO2 successfully and then resist quenching phenomenon, and the as-prepared AIS-CDs composites with a PLQY of 35% possess good air and thermal stability. Furthermore, we have successfully fabricated WLEDs with a high CRI and adjustable CCT through employing AIS-CDs nanocomposites as the down-conversion phosphors on a UV LED chip. The CIE of the WLEDs is located at (0.32,0.33), which is close to the pure white (0.33,0.33). Considering that both AIS QDs and CDs used in this strategy are Cd and Pb free, and abundant, this work might open up new avenues for the development of high-performance, low-cost, and eco-friendly WLEDs.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.5%), sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S·9H2O, 98.5%), Indium(III) chloride (InCl3, 99.9%), NH4OH (aqueous 5.0 M solution), zinc(II) acetate dihydrate (Zn(Ac)2·2H2O, 99.5%), glutathione (GSH, 99.5%), ethylenediamine, 2-propanol, ethanol, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99.5%), Poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), citric acid (CA) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin and used without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

AIS QDs are produced according to the previously reported procedure [23]. Typically, 2.5 mL deionized H2O and 2.4 mL 0.5 M aqueous GSH are added to 0.8 mL aqueous InCl3 (containing 0.25 M HNO3). During 5 min of vigorous stirring, 1 mL 5 M NH4OH solution is introduced. Then 2 mL aqueous 0.1 M AgNO3 is added followed by rapid addition of 1 mL 1 M aqueous Na2S·9H2O and 0.25 mL of 2 M CA. after that the resulting solution is kept at 98 °C for 60 min in a water bath. Subsequently, 5 mL of the homogeneous solution prepared from 3 mL 0.5 M aqueous GSH, 0.4 mL 5 M NH4OH solution, and 1.6 mL 1 M aqueous Zn(Ac)2·2H2O was introduced into the 10 mL of a obtained reddish-brown solution under a constant magnetic stirring. The colloidal solution is additionally heated at 98 °C for 10 min. The AIS QDs are rinsed with 2-propanol for purification.

The CDs are synthesized using a hydrothermally as reported in previous study [29]. Citric acid (1.051 g) and ethylenediamine (335 µL) are dissolved in deionized water (10 mL). Then, the solution is solvothermal heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The product is centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min) to obtain the CDs. After that, 1 mL CDs is dissolved in 10 mL deionized water for further use.

20 mg AIS QDs and 10 mg PVP are dissolved in 2 mL deionized water. The solution is sonicated for 30 min and dispersed in a mixture of ammonia solution (0.2 mL) and ethanol (8 mL). Afterward, 0.2 mL TEOS is added dropwise and stirred for 10 h. The resulting AIS@SiO2 are washed with water and ethanol for three times, and then dried at 65 °C for overnight in vacuum.

The CDs are deposited onto the surface of AIS@SiO2 by electrostatic attraction between APTES-functionalized silica surface and CDs. First of all, 50 mg AIS@SiO2 are dispersed into 5 mL ethanol through an ultrasonication treatment for 20 min. In order to obtain positively charged AIS@SiO2, 150 µL of APTES is added to the solution and followed stirred for 12 h. The APTES-functionalized AIS@SiO2 is washed by ethanol repeatedly to remove excess APTES. Subsequently, 1 mL Crude CDs solution was diluted in 20 mL deionized water; then, 1 mL diluted CDs aqueous solution and 20 mg APTES modified AIS@SiO2 were added into the 5 mL of deionized water, and stirred for 10 h. The resulting products are centrifuged (4000 rpm, 5 min) to obtain the AIS-CDs, and then washed by ethanol repeatedly to remove free CDs. The AIS-CDs are dried at 60 °C for 5 h in vacuum to form AIS-CDs powder. Noted that if we replace 1 mL diluted CDs aqueous solution with 0.42 mL diluted CDs, we can get another AIS-CDs nanocomposite.

AIS-CDs/PMMA with mass ratio of 4:1 are introduced into the acetone solution, and then sonicated for 20 min. After that, the mixture is coated onto the UV LED. Finally, the AIS-CDs powder-based WLEDs were obtained by a curing process at 50 °C for 60 mins.

The PL and absorption spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary Eclipse instrument and Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometry, respectively. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were recorded by a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope. XRD patterns were acquired by Shimadzu/6100 X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source. Fluorescence spectrofluorometer (F-7000, Hitachi) were used to measure the fluorescence lifetimes X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was obtained by an ESCA Lab220I-XL. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was collected on an IRPrestige-21 spectrophotometer. The PLQY measurements were carried out by Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer FS 5. The WLEDs were characterized on a PR-670 Spectra Scan spectrophotometer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2018YFB2200500); National Natural Science Foundation of China (61975023, 61520106012, 61674023, 61875211, 51702032); the Central Universities (2018CDYJSY0055, 106112017CDJQJ128837, 2019CDYGYB010, 2019CDYGYB019, 2018CDQYDL0051); the Young 1000 Talents Program of China, Strategic Priority Research Program of CAS (Grant No. XDB16030400), the Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology (cstc2018jszx-cyzdX0137); International S&T Cooperation Program of China (2016YFE0119300), Ministry of Industry and Information Technology “Green Manufacturing System Integration” Project (2018ZZCQ105), the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory of High Field Laser Physics (Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics), and the CAS Interdisciplinary Innovation Team.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.124654.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
